title: Rochdale Councillors Approve Pay Rise Amid Cost of Living Concerns
excerpt: Councillors in Rochdale have voted to increase their annual allowances despite ongoing cost of living challenges faced by residents.
content_html:
A vote at Rochdale Town Hall has resulted in an increase in annual allowances for councillors. The current allowance of £12,996.09 will rise to £13,515 following the decision made during a meeting on July 16.

The pay rise has sparked significant debate among councillors, particularly from members of the Reform and Workers Party, who opposed the increase. Councillor Mohammed Shafiq from the Workers Party described the pay rise as 'inappropriate' given the financial struggles many families in the borough are currently facing. Leader of Reform, Councillor John Taylor, echoed these sentiments, stating that it is not appropriate to approve such an increase during a cost of living crisis.

In response, Labour councillors defended the increase, arguing that the new allowance remains lower than those in other councils across Greater Manchester. Councillor Kath Bromfield stated that the increase would help make politics more accessible to those without substantial financial resources. Councillor Dylan Williams added that the new allowance works out to less than minimum wage considering the time commitment required from councillors.

Council leader Neil Emmott noted that those who opposed the rise were not obligated to claim the full amount. The increase applies only to the basic allowance, with all other special allowances remaining unchanged. Special allowances for various roles, such as the leader of the council and cabinet members, will not be affected by this decision.

title: Rochdale Council Plans Crackdown on Houses of Multiple Occupation
excerpt: Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) following recent protests in Heywood.
content_html:
Rochdale Council is set to crack down on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement stricter regulations. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood.

Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to introduce an Article 4 Direction to alleviate tensions surrounding HMOs. The issue was raised by Reform councillors during the latest town hall meeting. Article 4 directives would require all HMOs, regardless of size, to obtain specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people require such permission.

Councillor Lee Wolf from Reform expressed that while HMO licensing is important, it does not address the location of these properties or prevent excessive clustering. He noted that residents desire a greater say in the matter and believe that HMOs are impacting local services, particularly in specific areas.

The controlling Labour group indicated that it is already planning changes. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith thanked Reform for their support of the Labour plan. Fellow Labour councillor Richard Jackson added that they are actively working on the issue but must follow proper processes. He emphasised that implementing Article 4 requires substantial evidence and cannot be enacted immediately. The proposal is currently undergoing local authority processes and is expected to be presented to the full council for a formal vote later this year.

title: Man convicted of murdering teenager over stolen e-bike in Middleton
excerpt: Terrence King, 60, has been found guilty of murdering 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines, who stole an e-bike from another boy in Middleton last year.
content_html:
A man has been found guilty of murdering a teenager who stole an e-bike. Terrence King, 60, killed 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines by deliberately driving his car into him in Middleton, Greater Manchester, on 22 July 2025.

The jury heard that Simmonds-Caines had stolen the £3,000 bike from another boy on the Langley Estate. Following a fracas, he rode off on the e-bike, prompting King to pursue him in his vehicle.

King admitted to manslaughter but denied murder. However, after a two-week trial, he was convicted of murder and is scheduled to be sentenced on 23 July at Minshull Street Crown Court in Manchester.

The courtroom erupted with screams of joy from the victim's family and friends as the verdict was announced, with shouts of "thank you" heard from those present. CCTV footage captured the moments leading up to the incident, showing the events that unfolded before Simmonds-Caines was struck.

title: Albion Inn raises £1,090 for Rochdale MS Society
excerpt: The Albion Inn in Shawclough has raised £1,090 for the Rochdale branch of the MS Society following a community charity event.
summary: Customers, organisers and supporters at the Albion Inn came together to raise £1,090 for MS Society Rochdale, with the pub thanking Natasha Jones and Craig Jones for coordinating the event and donations.
content_html:
The Albion Inn in Shawclough has raised £1,090 for the Rochdale branch of the MS Society following a community charity event.

The pub announced the final total after donations and fundraising activities were brought together, thanking local residents and supporters for helping make the event a success.

Community effort raises four-figure total

The money was raised through a team effort involving customers, organisers and members of the wider community.

The Albion Inn gave special thanks to Natasha Jones and Craig Jones for organising the event programme and coordinating donations.

“Thank you once again to the whole community for your efforts. Great team effort by all.”

Supporting people affected by multiple sclerosis

The £1,090 donation will support MS Society Rochdale and its work with people affected by multiple sclerosis.

Multiple sclerosis is a condition affecting the brain and spinal cord and can cause a wide range of symptoms, including problems with movement, balance, vision and fatigue.

Local MS Society groups can provide information, social support and practical help for people living with the condition, as well as their families and carers.

Thanks to everyone who contributed

The pub thanked everyone who donated, attended or helped organise the fundraising effort.

The presentation image records the donation as £1,090 and is dated Saturday 11 July 2026.

This report is based on a public update and fundraising presentation image published by the Albion Inn following its charity event for MS Society Rochdale.

title: Lift Out of Service at Central Park Tram Stop
excerpt: The lift at Central Park tram stop towards Rochdale is currently out of service, affecting access for passengers.
content_html:

title: Reform UK pushes for borough-wide HMO controls across Rochdale
excerpt: Reform UK councillors have called for stronger planning controls on Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) across Rochdale Borough, arguing that residents should have a greater say over developments affecting their communities.
summary: A motion brought before Rochdale Council by Reform UK councillors has reignited debate over the growth of HMOs, with the party calling for borough-wide planning restrictions while welcoming council recognition that the issue requires further consideration.
content_html:
Reform UK councillors have renewed calls for stronger controls on Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) across Rochdale Borough following a debate at a recent Full Council meeting.

The party brought forward a motion proposing a borough-wide Article 4 Direction, which would remove certain permitted development rights and require planning permission before new HMOs could be established.

According to Reform UK, the proposal was intended to give residents a greater opportunity to comment on developments that may affect their neighbourhoods before approval is granted.

Debate over planning powers

During the council debate, the original motion was amended before being considered by councillors.

Reform UK said it would have preferred immediate borough-wide protections but ultimately supported the amended version after it formally acknowledged concerns surrounding increasing HMO concentrations and committed the council to further work on the potential use of Article 4 powers.

The party argued that rejecting the amended motion entirely would have risked losing momentum on an issue that has generated significant discussion among residents in several parts of the borough.

Concerns over community impact

Supporters of the proposal say growing concentrations of HMOs can place additional pressure on local infrastructure, parking, public services and community cohesion.

Reform UK councillors argue that the current planning framework allows many HMOs to be created without the level of scrutiny that residents would expect for other forms of development.

The party believes local people should have a greater voice when significant changes are proposed within established residential communities.

Borough-wide approach proposed

One of the central arguments made during the debate was that restrictions should apply consistently across the borough rather than being introduced only in selected locations.

Reform UK says protecting one neighbourhood while leaving others unaffected could simply relocate development pressure elsewhere.

The party therefore continues to support a borough-wide Article 4 Direction covering Rochdale, Middleton, Heywood and the Pennines.

Issue remains on the political agenda

The councillors say the debate represents an important first step in securing wider recognition of residents' concerns.

While acknowledging that the outcome did not fully reflect their original proposal, Reform UK believes the discussion has ensured that HMO policy remains a live political issue within Rochdale Borough.

The council is expected to continue gathering evidence and reviewing potential planning options before any future decisions are made regarding Article 4 powers.

This article is based on statements published by Reform UK Rochdale Councillors following a Full Council debate regarding HMOs and planning policy. The views expressed are those of the party and form part of an ongoing political discussion concerning housing and planning within Rochdale Borough.

title: Mayoral candidate raises alarm over intoxicated children and predatory behaviour in Manchester
excerpt: Greater Manchester mayoral candidate Marlon Scott West has raised serious concerns after a late-night visit to Piccadilly Gardens revealed children appearing intoxicated, suspected drug use, anti-social behaviour and inappropriate approaches towards women.
summary: Marlon Scott West and Montgomery Toms visited Piccadilly Gardens at night and highlighted scenes involving young people apparently under the influence of alcohol or drugs, alongside behaviour by groups of men which campaigners described as predatory and potentially criminal.
content_html:
Greater Manchester mayoral candidate Marlon Scott West has raised serious concerns about public safety after witnessing troubling scenes during a late-night visit to Piccadilly Gardens.

West visited the busy Manchester city-centre location alongside campaigner Montgomery Toms to examine the conditions faced by residents, visitors and commuters after dark.

The footage recorded during the visit showed young people who appeared to be children visibly affected by alcohol or other substances, alongside suspected public drug use, disorder and widespread anti-social behaviour.

Children appeared intoxicated in public

One of the most concerning aspects of the visit was the presence of young people who appeared to be under the age of 18 and heavily intoxicated.

The campaigners questioned why children were able to remain in such a vulnerable condition in a major public space without apparent intervention from responsible adults or public services.

They argued that visibly intoxicated children in a city-centre environment could be at significant risk of exploitation, violence, accidental injury or becoming involved in criminal activity.

Inappropriate and predatory behaviour towards women

The visit also highlighted repeated approaches towards women by groups of men, with behaviour described by those present as inappropriate, intimidating and predatory.

Campaigners said some of the conduct witnessed went beyond behaviour that could simply be described as uncomfortable and appeared to border on criminal harassment or public-order offending.

The men involved were described by the campaigners as foreign nationals. Rochdale Daily has not independently established the identity, nationality or immigration status of any individual shown.

No allegation of a specific criminal offence should be inferred against an identifiable person unless confirmed following an investigation by Greater Manchester Police.

Suspected drug use and alcohol-related disorder

West and Toms also reported witnessing suspected drug use, people heavily under the influence of alcohol and behaviour that disrupted the safety and accessibility of the area.

They said the conditions were unacceptable for one of Greater Manchester’s most prominent public spaces and transport hubs.

Piccadilly Gardens is used by thousands of commuters and visitors each day, including people travelling into Manchester from Rochdale, Middleton, Heywood and the Pennines.

Calls for intervention and stronger enforcement

West said the scenes demonstrated a need for more visible policing, stronger enforcement and clearer intervention where children, vulnerable people or women may be at risk.

The campaign also called for greater scrutiny of how anti-social behaviour, suspected drug activity, public intoxication and threatening conduct are managed in Manchester city centre.

Campaigners argued that support services for addiction, homelessness and vulnerable young people must operate alongside effective enforcement against intimidation, harassment and criminal behaviour.

Public safety becomes mayoral election issue

The visit forms part of West’s campaign to become the next Mayor of Greater Manchester.

Policing, safeguarding, public-order enforcement and the condition of town and city centres are expected to remain prominent issues during the mayoral election campaign.

West said residents should be able to travel through Greater Manchester, use public transport and enjoy the city’s public spaces without encountering intoxicated children, open drug use, predatory behaviour or serious disorder.

This report is based on footage and campaign material supplied following a late-night visit by Marlon Scott West and Montgomery Toms to Piccadilly Gardens. Descriptions of intoxication, suspected drug use, predatory conduct and potentially criminal behaviour reflect what was shown in the footage and the observations made by those present. Rochdale Daily has not independently confirmed the identity, age, nationality, immigration status or criminal conduct of any identifiable individual.

title: Police report made after £380 allegedly stolen from locker at Rochdale Leisure Centre
excerpt: A Rochdale mother says £380 was taken from her purse after a locker key went missing while she was swimming with her children at Rochdale Leisure Centre.
summary: The alleged theft has been reported to police after a visitor said her locker was searched and £380 removed. Leisure-centre staff are also said to have reviewed CCTV footage showing two people of potential interest.
content_html:
A police report has been made after a visitor said £380 was stolen from her locker while she was swimming with her children at Rochdale Leisure Centre.

Sarah-Jane Johnson said she had removed the locker key from her wrist while playing with one of her children and left it beside the pool for no more than 10 minutes.

When the family later left the pool, she said the key could not be found and the contents of the locker appeared to have been searched.

Cash allegedly taken from purse

Johnson said £380 had been removed from her purse. She had reportedly intended to pay the money into a Post Office account the following day.

She described feeling distressed and violated following the incident.

“You just don’t expect something like this to happen when you’re at the swimming baths having a good time with your children.”

CCTV footage reviewed

According to Johnson’s public account, leisure-centre staff reviewed CCTV after the alleged theft and found footage showing two people who may be able to assist with enquiries.

She said images of their faces were available and that the incident had been reported to Greater Manchester Police.

Rochdale Daily has not independently viewed the footage or received confirmation of any identification, arrest or charge.

Warning to other visitors

Johnson urged people using Rochdale Leisure Centre to keep their locker keys with them throughout their visit.

Visitors should also avoid leaving cash or other valuable belongings unattended and should notify staff immediately if a key or access token goes missing.

Report What You Know

Information about this incident: Anyone who witnessed suspicious activity or has relevant information should report it through the Greater Manchester Police online reporting service or call 101.

Anonymous information: Contact Crimestoppers anonymously on 0800 555 111 or submit information anonymously online.

Emergency: Call 999 if a theft is in progress, a suspect remains at the scene or someone is in immediate danger.

This is an ongoing report based on a public account published by Sarah-Jane Johnson. The allegations have not yet been independently confirmed by Greater Manchester Police or Rochdale Leisure Centre. Further verified information will be added when available.

title: Healey councillor welcomes first beehive to Number One Riverside roof
excerpt: Healey ward councillor Tricia Ayrton has welcomed the installation of the first beehive on the roof of Number One Riverside in Rochdale town centre.
summary: A new rooftop beehive supplied and maintained by Boro Bees will make use of the sedum roof and nearby riverbank wildflowers, with its first honey being left for the colony over winter.
content_html:
Healey ward councillor Tricia Ayrton has welcomed the installation of the first beehive on the roof of Number One Riverside in Rochdale town centre.

The hive was installed with support from Boro Bees, which supplied the colony and will continue to maintain it.

Councillor Ayrton described the installation as exciting news and said the bees would benefit from the building’s sedum roof and the wildflowers growing along the nearby riverbank.

Supporting bees in the town centre

The rooftop location will give the bees access to pollen and nectar from plants around Number One Riverside, the River Roch and areas further afield.

Sedum roofs are planted with low-growing vegetation and can provide additional habitats and food sources for pollinating insects in built-up areas.

The project adds a new biodiversity feature to the council building while demonstrating how urban spaces can be adapted to support wildlife.

First honey to remain with the colony

Any honey produced during the colony’s first year will be left for the bees themselves.

This will help the colony establish itself and provide the food reserves needed to survive and remain healthy through the winter.

Those involved in the project hope the hive may produce surplus honey in future years once the colony is well established.

“The bees will benefit from the sedum roof on the top of Number One Riverside and from the wildflowers that grow along the riverbank.”

Hive to be maintained by local specialists

Boro Bees will be responsible for maintaining the hive and monitoring the health of the colony.

Photographs from the installation show a beekeeper placing frames containing the bees inside the wooden rooftop hive.

This report is based on a public update and photographs published by Healey ward councillor Tricia Ayrton following the installation of the beehive at Number One Riverside.

title: Middleton councillors explore new measures to improve town-centre safety
excerpt: Reform UK councillors in Middleton are considering a cycling dismount zone, a review of CCTV provision and wider community-safety measures aimed at making the town centre safer and more welcoming.
summary: Councillors say they are gathering advice from highways, legal and community-safety officers before deciding whether new restrictions or improvements should be introduced in Middleton town centre.
content_html:
Middleton councillors are exploring a range of measures designed to improve pedestrian safety and make the town centre a more welcoming place for residents, visitors and businesses.

Reform UK councillors elected in May said they have been examining whether a cycling dismount zone could be introduced in parts of Middleton town centre.

The proposal is intended to address concerns about anti-social behaviour involving bicycles while improving safety for people walking through busy areas.

Focus on practical and enforceable measures

Councillors said an important part of the work will be establishing whether any new restriction could be enforced effectively.

They are seeking advice from highways, legal and community-safety officers before any decision is made.

The group said it wants to ensure that any action introduced is practical, proportionate and capable of making a visible difference.

Review of CCTV provision

The councillors are also calling for a review of the town centre’s CCTV network.

They said residents should be able to understand how public-safety infrastructure is being used and whether it is helping to deter anti-social behaviour, support enforcement and improve confidence in the town centre.

The review could consider how cameras are monitored, how footage is used and whether the current network is delivering the intended outcomes.

Creating a welcoming town centre

The statement highlights concerns involving bicycles, e-bikes and e-scooters, while stressing the wider goal of creating a safe and positive environment for everyone.

Councillors said Middleton town centre should be a place where people feel comfortable spending time, shopping and supporting local businesses.

They are continuing to gather information before bringing forward any formal proposals.

Residents invited to contribute

Residents, businesses, pedestrians and cyclists are being invited to share constructive feedback as the work develops.

The councillors said local experiences will be important in shaping measures that balance pedestrian safety, accessibility and the needs of people travelling through the town centre.

This report is based on a public statement issued by Reform UK councillors in Rochdale borough regarding anti-social behaviour and safety in Middleton town centre. No final policy decision has yet been made.

title: Leader of Rochdale grooming gang released from prison as government considers deportation
excerpt: Recent media reports have reignited discussions about grooming gangs in Rochdale, prompting responses from Scottish officials regarding the differences in child protection measures.
content_html:
Recent media coverage has brought the issue of grooming gangs in Rochdale back into the spotlight. Reports suggest that some outlets are attempting to draw parallels between incidents in England and Scotland, despite significant differences in the nature of these cases.

Scottish officials have responded to these claims, emphasising that there are currently no investigations in Scotland that mirror the organised gang grooming observed in England. Police Scotland has confirmed that they are not aware of any similar offences against children in Scotland.

The First Minister has highlighted the importance of understanding the specific legislative and regulatory landscape in Scotland, noting that while child sexual abuse is a serious concern, it does not necessarily involve the same organised gang structures seen in some English cases. The First Minister also referenced a recent case of child sexual abuse in Glasgow, clarifying that it is subject to a learning review and differs from the organised gang grooming reported in England.

In Scotland, all healthcare and education professionals are mandated to report any suspicions of child abuse, including sexual exploitation. Failure to do so could result in disciplinary action or civil claims against practitioners. All care staff must also be registered in Scotland, which is a measure not uniformly applied in England.

title: Rochdale Students Win National Award for Online Safety Film
excerpt: Students from Skills 4 All in Rochdale have secured second place in the secondary category of the 2026 Childnet Film Competition with a film on the responsible use of artificial intelligence.
content_html:
A group of students from Skills 4 All in Rochdale has won second place in the secondary category of the 2026 Childnet Film Competition. Their film addresses the safe and responsible use of artificial intelligence (AI).

The awards were announced at Disney headquarters in Hammersmith during the 17th annual Childnet Film Competition, which invites young people across the UK to create short films promoting online safety. This year's theme was 'Smart tech, safe choices', focusing on the implications of AI in everyday life. The students' film is set in a dystopian version of Manchester, illustrating the potential consequences of over-reliance on AI.

The judging panel, which included representatives from the BBC, the British Board of Film Classification, Disney, and the Motion Picture Association, commended the film for its creativity and engaging storytelling. As runners-up, the students will receive professional filmmaking equipment, and their film will be classified by the BBFC, allowing it to be screened in cinemas across the UK.

Will Gardner OBE, chief executive of Childnet, highlighted the importance of educating young people about the responsible use of AI, noting that 97% of children aged 8 to 17 have used AI in some form. Charlotte Tudor, vice president of marketing for Disney, expressed pride in supporting the initiative, emphasising the need for safe online experiences.

title: Rochdale marks national day of memory for victims of honour-based abuse
excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has joined communities across the country in remembering victims of honour-based abuse and highlighting support available to people experiencing domestic violence.
summary: The National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse is marked on 14 July, the birthday of Shafilea Ahmed, whose death became one of the UK’s most widely recognised cases of so-called honour-based killing.
content_html:
Rochdale Borough Council has joined communities across the country in marking the National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse.

The annual day of remembrance takes place on 14 July and provides an opportunity to remember those whose lives have been lost through so-called honour-based abuse and to call for collective action to prevent further harm.

Remembering Shafilea Ahmed

The date was chosen because 14 July would have been the birthday of Shafilea Ahmed, a young woman whose life was taken after she was perceived to have brought shame upon her family.

Her case has become closely associated with national efforts to raise awareness of honour-based abuse, forced marriage and the risks faced by people subjected to family or community control.

Rochdale Borough Council said an estimated 12 lives are lost to so-called honour killings each year, although the true number is believed to be higher.

Support available in Rochdale

The council has reminded residents that its domestic abuse service supports people of any background or gender who are affected by domestic violence or abuse.

Support is also available for individuals who want help to change abusive behaviour.

Residents can contact the Rochdale domestic abuse service on 0161 507 9609 or visit the Rochdale Borough Council domestic violence and abuse support page.

Anyone who is in immediate danger should call 999.

Mental-health support

People who need urgent mental-health advice can call NHS 111 and select the mental-health option where available.

Information about local services is also available through the council’s mental-health support directory.

This report is based on a public statement issued by Rochdale Borough Council to mark the National Day of Memory for Victims of Honour-Based Abuse on 14 July 2026.

In response, Oldham council acknowledged the concerns regarding the potholes and confirmed that their highways team has inspected the area. They stated that the site is being considered for future planned repair works, subject to priorities across the borough.

title: Rochdale gang leader relocated after address disclosed online
excerpt: Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has outlined plans to deport Shabir Ahmed, the leader of the Rochdale grooming gang, following his release from prison after serving 14 years for multiple sexual offences.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 18 July 2026 at 05:12. 47 source updates have been combined into this article.

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has outlined plans to deport Shabir Ahmed, the leader of the Rochdale grooming gang, following his release from prison after serving 14 years for multiple sexual offences.

Latest update

The UK government is seeking the deportation of a man convicted of rape in Rochdale, but Pakistan has refused to accept him back. This situation has led to a diplomatic standoff between the two countries.

The man, whose identity has not been disclosed, was sentenced for his crimes in the UK. Authorities are pressing for his removal, citing public safety concerns.

Pakistan's refusal to accept the deportation request has raised questions about the handling of such cases and the cooperation between the two nations on criminal matters.

The UK government continues to explore options to resolve the situation, but the outcome remains uncertain as diplomatic discussions are ongoing.

Earlier developments

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has outlined plans to deport Shabir Ahmed, the leader of the Rochdale grooming gang, following his release from prison after serving 14 years for multiple sexual offences.

The UK government is set to introduce changes to immigration laws aimed at enabling the deportation of Shabir Ahmed, the leader of the Rochdale grooming gang. Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood announced plans to amend the Immigration and Asylum Bill, which would remove legal protections preventing Ahmed's deportation due to his long-term residency in the UK.

Ahmed, who was convicted in 2012 for multiple counts of child rape and sexual offences, was released from prison on 2 July after serving 14 years of a 22-year sentence. Following his release, there was significant public outcry regarding his placement in Accrington, where he was housed near a playground and a mosque. Local MP Sarah Smith expressed her disgust at his initial placement and confirmed he has since been relocated outside the borough.

The government has indicated that Pakistan could face visa restrictions if it does not agree to take back Ahmed, who has been stripped of his British citizenship. However, Pakistan has publicly stated it will not accept him back, complicating the UK’s efforts to deport him. The Foreign Office has emphasised that the deportation plans are an internal matter for the UK, despite the diplomatic tensions arising from the situation.

Councillors in Rochdale have unanimously called for Ahmed's deportation, highlighting the need to close the legal loophole that currently prevents his removal. The proposed changes to the law are expected to be debated in the Commons soon, but it remains uncertain whether Pakistan will cooperate in facilitating Ahmed's return.

Pakistan has blamed the UK for allowing Shabir Ahmed, the leader of the Rochdale grooming gang, to commit numerous sexual offences against girls. Tahir Andrabi, a spokesman for Pakistan's Foreign Office, claimed that the causes of Ahmed's criminality lie in the UK, where he was raised and groomed.

Ahmed, 73, was released from prison on licence two weeks ago after serving 14 years of a 22-year sentence for 30 child rape offences. The UK government is seeking to deport him back to Pakistan, threatening visa restrictions if the country does not accept him. However, Andrabi stated that Pakistan has no connection to the matter and insisted that Ahmed must be dealt with under UK laws.

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has outlined new laws that could facilitate Ahmed's deportation. However, it remains uncertain whether Pakistan will agree to take him back, as the country has previously expressed reluctance to accept him due to his long absence from Pakistan.

In a statement, the First Minister of Scotland addressed calls for a national review of child sexual abuse cases, emphasising the need to consider the specific legislative and regulatory context in Scotland. The First Minister noted that while there have been serious cases of child sexual abuse, they differ from the organised gang grooming observed in England. Police Scotland has confirmed that there are currently no investigations in Scotland that mirror the cases described in recent reports regarding grooming gangs. This distinction is crucial in understanding the nature of child protection efforts in both regions.

The First Minister's comments also highlighted the responsibilities of healthcare and education professionals in Scotland, who are mandated to report any suspected child abuse. Failure to do so could result in disciplinary action or removal from professional registers. All care staff must be registered in Scotland, which is a significant aspect of the child protection framework.

The recent media focus has raised awareness of the differences in how child sexual abuse is addressed in Scotland compared to England. While the reports have sparked discussions, officials stress the importance of understanding the unique context of each region's approach to safeguarding children.

content_html:
Police are appealing for information after a woman died following a collision on the M62 near Rochdale.

Greater Manchester Police said officers were called at around 11pm on Saturday 11 July following reports of a collision involving a grey Nissan Juke and a grey BMW X5.

The incident happened between junctions 21 and 22 of the motorway.

Woman died at the scene

The driver of the Nissan, a woman aged in her 50s, suffered serious injuries in the collision.

Despite the efforts of emergency services, she was pronounced dead at the scene.

No further information about the woman has been released in the police appeal.

Greater Manchester Police’s Serious Collision Investigation Unit is investigating the circumstances surrounding the collision.

Officers want to hear from anyone who witnessed the incident or who may have dashcam or CCTV footage recorded in the area at around the time it happened.

Anyone who can assist should contact the investigation team on 0161 856 4741, quoting log 4349 of 11 July 2026.

Report online: Information can also be submitted through the Greater Manchester Police reporting service.

title: Pionero Lounge raises more than £2,100 for Springhill Hospice
excerpt: Customers and staff at Pionero Lounge in Rochdale have raised £2,154 for Springhill Hospice through a month of fundraising events and community activities.
summary: Pionero Lounge has announced that its LoungeAid fundraising campaign raised £2,154 for Springhill Hospice, with support coming from customers, volunteers and the wider Rochdale community.
content_html:
Pionero Lounge has announced that it has raised £2,154 for Springhill Hospice following a month of fundraising activities throughout June.

The Rochdale venue revealed the total after taking part in LoungeAid, a charity fundraising campaign that saw customers and staff come together to support the local hospice.

“We did it! Our final total fundraised is £2,154.”

Community comes together

The fundraising total was achieved through a variety of events and activities held during June.

Customers supported the campaign by making donations, attending bingo sessions, taking part in pottery painting activities, joining quiz nights and contributing through collection pots.

Pionero Lounge thanked everyone who got involved and said every contribution, no matter how small, helped make a difference.

Supporting Springhill Hospice

According to information shared by the venue, around 70 pence of every pound raised helps fund patient care and support services provided by Springhill Hospice.

The remaining funds help maintain facilities and ensure the hospice remains a safe, welcoming environment for patients and their families.

Springhill Hospice provides specialist palliative and end-of-life care for people across Rochdale and the surrounding area, with services available free of charge to those who need them.

Thank you to Rochdale residents

Pionero Lounge said the success of the campaign reflected the generosity of the local community and thanked everyone who attended events or made donations.

The venue described the fundraising effort as a true community achievement and praised customers for helping to support a charity that plays a vital role across the borough.

This report is based on a public Facebook update published by Pionero Lounge following its June LoungeAid fundraising campaign for Springhill Hospice.

title: Rochdale residents warned over open-water dangers and moorland fires
excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has urged residents to avoid entering open water and to keep barbecues, fires and fireworks away from the countryside during hot, dry weather.
summary: The council has issued summer safety warnings highlighting the dangers of open-water swimming and the risk that even a small flame could start a serious moorland fire.
content_html:
Residents across Rochdale borough are being urged to take extra care around open water and on the moors during periods of hot and dry weather.

Rochdale Borough Council has shared separate warnings about the risk of drowning and the danger of fires spreading rapidly through dry countryside.

Warning against entering open water

A water-safety campaign shared by the council warns people not to encourage friends to enter reservoirs, lakes, rivers or other open water.

“If you encourage your mates to go in the water it could be the last time you see them.”

Open water can contain hazards that are not visible from the surface, including sudden changes in depth, cold temperatures, currents and submerged objects.

The warning was produced with the Water Safety Partnership as part of efforts to prevent avoidable deaths and serious incidents.

No fires, barbecues or fireworks on moorland

The council has also reminded visitors that even a small flame can trigger a moorland fire during a prolonged dry spell.

People visiting the countryside are being told not to take disposable barbecues or camping stoves onto the moors and not to start open fires.

Visitors should also make sure cigarettes are fully extinguished and placed in a bin, while fireworks and sky lanterns should not be used in the countryside.

Council campaign material states that offences involving fires, barbecues or fireworks may result in a fine of up to £2,500 and, in some circumstances, a prison sentence.

What to do if a fire starts

Anyone who sees a fire should move away from the affected area and call 999 immediately.

People should not attempt to tackle a spreading moorland fire themselves, as conditions can change quickly and smoke can make it difficult to escape safely.

The council has asked residents and visitors to respect the countryside and help protect local wildlife, habitats and nearby communities.

This report is based on public safety material published by Rochdale Borough Council and the Water Safety Partnership.

title: Middleton Big Dig uncovers evidence of medieval Tonge Hall
excerpt: More than 350 people, including pupils from five local schools, took part in the Middleton Big Dig and uncovered evidence of activity dating from the medieval period.
summary: The community archaeological dig uncovered medieval ridge-and-furrow features and structures believed to relate to an earlier hall that stood on the Tonge Hall site before the existing Tudor building.
content_html:
Evidence of medieval farming and structures connected with an earlier version of Tonge Hall has been uncovered during a community archaeological dig in Middleton.

More than 350 people took part in the Middleton Big Dig, including pupils from five local schools.

Rochdale Borough Council said participants found evidence of activity spanning from the medieval period to the present day.

Medieval landscape discovered

Among the discoveries was evidence of ridge-and-furrow farming, a landscape feature created by historic ploughing methods widely used during the Middle Ages.

Archaeologists and volunteers also uncovered structures believed to relate to the original medieval hall that existed before the Tudor building now standing on the site.

The surviving building is known as Tonge Hall.

More than 350 people involved

The project involved residents, volunteers and children from five schools, giving young people the opportunity to take part in practical archaeology and learn more about Middleton’s history.

Pupils from St Peter’s RC Primary School were among those who visited the site and helped examine finds uncovered during the dig.

Photographs shared by the council show pupils working alongside members of the archaeological team and examining material recovered from the excavation area.

Finds to be cleaned and catalogued

The objects and other material discovered during the project will now be cleaned, recorded and catalogued.

Once that work has been completed, the finds are expected to be offered to Touchstones Rochdale for inclusion in its collection.

The dig formed part of the activity programme supporting the restoration of the Broadfield Park Slopes.

The wider project has received funding from the National Lottery Heritage Fund.

This report is based on a public update and photographs published by Rochdale Borough Council following the completion of the Middleton Big Dig.

title: Petition calls for defibrillators to be mandatory in all gyms
excerpt: A parliamentary petition is calling for every gym and exercise establishment to be legally required to have a defibrillator available.
summary: The petition, created by Eleanor Felton-Page, asks the Government to make automated external defibrillators mandatory in all gyms and exercise venues. It is due to close on 13 October 2026.
content_html:
A parliamentary petition is calling for defibrillators to become a legal requirement in every gym and exercise establishment.

The petition was created by Eleanor Felton-Page and is open for signatures until 13 October 2026.

It asks the Government to require gyms and other exercise venues to make an automated external defibrillator, commonly known as an AED, readily available in case somebody suffers a cardiac arrest.

Call for quicker access to lifesaving equipment

The petition argues that rapid access to a defibrillator could improve a person's chances of survival when cardiac arrest happens during or shortly after exercise.

Its supporting statement claims that more than 90% of sudden cardiac arrests involving young athletes occur during or immediately after exercise.

It also states that using an AED within three to five minutes could increase survival rates significantly. These figures are claims presented by the petition organiser and have not been independently verified by Rochdale Daily.

What is a defibrillator?

An automated external defibrillator is a portable device designed to analyse a person's heart rhythm and, where appropriate, deliver an electric shock to help restore a normal rhythm.

The devices provide spoken or visual instructions and can be used by members of the public while emergency help is on its way.

Anyone who believes a person is in cardiac arrest should call 999 immediately, begin CPR when instructed and send somebody to locate the nearest defibrillator where possible.

How to sign the petition

People wishing to support the proposal can sign the petition on the UK Parliament website.

Under the parliamentary petitions system, petitions that reach 10,000 signatures normally receive a Government response, while those reaching 100,000 signatures are considered for debate in Parliament.

This report is based on the wording of UK Parliament petition 764204. Medical and statistical claims attributed to the petition reflect the organiser's published case for the proposal.

title: Pupils quiz Paul Waugh about Parliament during Littleborough school visit
excerpt: Pupils at St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough questioned Paul Waugh about Parliament during a special assembly ahead of England’s match.
summary: Paul Waugh visited St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough, where pupils wearing England shirts took part in an assembly about Parliament and the history of democracy.
content_html:
Pupils at St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough welcomed Paul Waugh for a special assembly about Parliament and democracy.

Children asked questions about how Parliament works and discussed England’s and Rochdale’s history of democratic participation.

The visit took place ahead of England’s match on Saturday evening, with many pupils attending the assembly in England football shirts and other red-and-white clothing.

Questions about Parliament

Waugh thanked headteacher Mrs Ruane, school staff and pupils for the welcome and for the questions raised during the session.

Photographs shared following the visit showed pupils taking part in a group discussion before listening to a presentation titled All About Parliament.

The session gave children an opportunity to learn more about political representation, democratic institutions and the role Parliament plays in national life.

England colours at school

The school community also marked the occasion by wearing England tops ahead of the evening’s football fixture.

Waugh said it was good to see the pupils showing their support and expressed hope that they had learned more about the country’s democratic history during the visit.
